page: Amazon Web Services c1.medium is a legacy first-generation compute-optimized instance. It is powered by an Intel Xeon E5-2651 v2 processor operating at 1.8 GHz, delivering 2 dedicated physical cores and 2 vCPUs. This instance utilizes the legacy i386 CPU architecture and runs on the Xen hypervisor. It is equipped with 2.0 GB of system memory, which equates to 0.85 GB of RAM per core. Local storage is bundled as a 350 GB HDD, and the baseline network bandwidth is rated at 0 Gbps. No GPU accelerators are present.

Performance benchmarks place the c1.medium in the lower tiers of modern cloud hardware. Its stress-ng single-core CPU performance is categorized as weak, while multi-core CPU performance and memory bandwidth tests fall into the bottom 10%. The legacy processor lacks modern instruction sets such as AVX, AVX-512, and AMX, limiting its capability for vector and matrix calculations. However, it does support hardware-accelerated AES cryptography.

From a qualitative cost-efficiency perspective, the c1.medium represents an older hardware generation with low resource density and low performance-per-resource ratios compared to modern instances. It is best suited for legacy 32-bit applications, basic static web serving, or lightweight background utility tasks that require local HDD storage but do not demand high memory or modern CPU instruction sets.
description: Amazon Web Services c1.medium is a legacy first-generation compute-optimized instance utilizing the i386 architecture. Powered by an Intel Xeon E5-2651 v2 processor running at 1.8 GHz, it features 2 dedicated physical cores, 2 vCPUs, and 2.0 GB of system memory. The instance includes a bundled 350 GB local HDD but lacks GPU acceleration and baseline network bandwidth. Performance benchmarks, including Geekbench and stress-ng, consistently place this instance in the bottom 10% to 25% of modern hardware. The absence of modern instruction sets like AVX and AVX-512 further limits its computational efficiency. Qualitatively, the c1.medium offers low resource density and is best reserved for legacy 32-bit workloads, basic static web serving, or lightweight utility tasks requiring local HDD storage.

tagline: A legacy 32-bit compute instance featuring dedicated cores and local HDD storage for basic utility workloads.
